data.title: Trump And The Global Chessboard
data.description:
By Samuel Rines

Markets often react sharply to geopolitical developments, but just as often, they overreact. Investors today are grappling with heightened global tensions, rising oil prices and uncertainty around central bank policy. The key question is whether these risks meaningfully alter the economic outlook or simply create short-term volatility.

Recent discussions across fixed income, equities and macro strategy suggest a more measured interpretation; while headlines are noisy, the underlying fundamentals remain relatively stable.

Geopolitics: Important but Not Determinative

Geopolitical events can drive sudden market moves, particularly in commodities and safe-haven assets. However, not all conflicts translate into prolonged economic damage.

From a macro perspective, the current environment appears more consistent with targeted, short-duration engagement rather than an extended military commitment. That distinction matters. Markets tend to price in worst-case scenarios early, but when escalation proves limited, risk assets often stabilize.

For investors, this reinforces an important principle: geopolitical volatility does not automatically equate to structural economic deterioration.

Fixed Income: Finding Value After the Sell-Off

Bond markets have experienced notable swings, with Treasury yields pushing higher before retracing. This volatility reflects shifting expectations around growth, inflation and Federal Reserve policy.

Yet the broader outlook remains intact:

  • Growth is expected to remain modest, not recessionary
  • Inflation is expected to remain somewhat sticky
  • The Fed is likely near the end of its rate cycle

At higher yield levels, Treasuries have begun to attract buyers again, suggesting that valuation is reasserting itself as a key driver. Rather than signaling distress, recent moves may represent a normalization process.

Equity Markets: Looking Through the Noise

Equity investors are balancing multiple crosscurrents—oil prices, interest rates and global uncertainty. However, comparisons to prior periods offer useful context.

The current environment shares similarities with the early 2010s:

  • Elevated energy prices
  • External macro concerns
  • Continued economic expansion

Importantly, today’s backdrop includes additional fiscal support, which may help cushion potential headwinds. This makes the case for a deep or imminent recession less compelling.

Central Banks and the Currency Opportunity

One of the most compelling themes emerging is the divergence between market expectations and likely policy outcomes.

Consensus views suggest:

  • The Federal Reserve remains on hold
  • The European Central Bank and Bank of Japan may tighten policy further

However, this narrative appears overly aggressive. Structural challenges—such as a cooling housing market in the U.S. and stress signals in private credit—argue against a renewed tightening cycle globally.

If expected rate hikes abroad fail to materialize:

  • The euro and yen could weaken
  • The U.S. dollar could strengthen

Adding to this setup is positioning. Investor sentiment has recently skewed heavily bearish on the dollar, creating the conditions for a contrarian upside move.

Strategy and Portfolio Implications

For investors, the key is to separate signals from noise:

  • Maintain exposure to high-quality fixed income as yields become more attractive
  • Avoid overreacting to geopolitical headlines that may not have lasting economic impact
  • Consider currency positioning, particularly the potential for U.S. dollar strength

Rather than chasing short-term narratives, portfolios may benefit from aligning with underlying macro trends and valuation opportunities.

Conclusion

Periods of uncertainty often feel more consequential than they ultimately prove to be. While geopolitical developments and policy debates dominate headlines, the foundation of the global economy remains relatively steady.

For investors, the opportunity lies in staying disciplined—recognizing that volatility can create entry points, not just risks. In today’s environment, that means focusing on resilient growth, attractive fixed income yields and a potentially underappreciated U.S. dollar.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy”.

data.title: Temporary Disruption - Or The Start Of A Global Supply Shock?
data.description:
Global recession risks are rising. The conflict involving Iran – and the associated disruption of energy and other cargo shipments through the Strait of Hormuz – has now entered its fifth week. Global markets have remained relatively calm, despite shipping disruptions that threaten roughly 20% of the world’s energy supply. That share reflects the volume of oil and energy products that typically move from Middle East producers through the strait to global importing markets. While the markets continue to price a near-term resolution, and elevated global inventories will for a time insulate economies, the economic costs will build as these disruptions persist. Eventually, and perhaps sooner rather than later, the risk is that markets, which have initially focused on the temporary inflationary effects of this crisis – and priced monetary policy tightening across DM rates markets – will have to eventually contend with greater global recession risks and demand destruction that could weigh on equity and credit markets and increase the premium investors place on bonds as a perceived safe store of value.

Unprecedented disruption

A period of disruption on the order of 20% of global oil supplies would be unprecedented in modern history.To put the disruptions in context, the decline in global consumption of oil and energy products during the peak COVID‑related shutdowns was also roughly 20%, according to data from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). During the same period in the first half of 2020, global GDP contracted over 10% on an annualized basis. Both global GDP and oil consumption recovered quickly as economic activity restarted.

Other large historical global oil production disruptions include the Arab oil embargo and Iranian revolution in early and late 1970s. At their peak, those disruptions accounted for roughly 5% to 7% of global oil production decline, according to data from the International Energy Association, and coincided with U.S. recessions and sharp slowdowns in global growth. The early 1990s Gulf War also accounted for an 8%-10% production disruption, with real GDP growth across OECD countries falling from 3.6% to 1.4%.

Pricing a near-term resolution

What explains the relative market calm of broader global financial markets in the current episode? We think a reasonable explanation is that market participants expect the disruptions to be temporary. The prices of contracts for future oil delivery illustrate the temporary nature of the shock currently priced into markets. Despite a $125/barrel price of physical barrels changing hands today in the North Sea (i.e., Brent “dated” price), futures contracts for December 2026 delivery were trading at $80/barrel as of the time of this writing – a significant discount.

The world was also enjoying a glut of oil inventories before the conflict, and as a result, entered this episode with inventory buffers outside the Middle East that will, for a time, insulate a disruption in oil flows. It is also notable that Middle East storage capacity has allowed oil production to continue despite producers’ inability to transport it out of the region. The blockage of roughly 20 million barrels a day of oil and energy products that previously passed through the strait has so far resultedinthe shutdown of a little over 10 million barrels a day of production, with the remainder continuing and filling up storage tanks.

Increasing risk of a prolonged supply disruption. Expectations for a relatively fast resolution, combined with these economic buffers, have likely limited the extent of the financial conditions tightening. However, buffers do not last forever, and as the conflict and waterway closure continue, global markets must increasingly consider how long until this shifts to a genuine negative supply shock, not merely a price‑driven redistribution of income between energy producers and consumers. Shipping, logistics and storage capacity considerations are all important factors.

On shipping and logistics timeframes, with the last tankers leaving the Strait of Hormuz in late February, the final cargoes are only now reaching their destinations. According to industry experts, it takes roughly 10-20 days for Persian Gulf cargos to reach Asia; those regions are already dealing with the immediate effects of production disruptions. Asia is followed by Europe and Africa, with shipping times of approximately 20-35 days, and then the U.S. Gulf Coast at roughly 35-45 days travel.

There are inventory buffers outside of the Middle East; however, the extent of those buffers is uneven across regions, and high quality data availability – particularly for China – adds uncertainty around precise timing. IEA estimates that oil and product inventories across OECD countries, both owned commercially and by governments, could last for roughly 140 days at last year’s demand levels. But large variation exists across countries, with several countries, including Mexico, Australia, Ireland and the UK, having less than two months of inventory, according to the IEA.

India and Australia, for example, are already implementing policies to address emerging shortages. Asian refiners have also pre-emptively reduced throughput and production in an effort to smooth through the disruption without fully shutting down operations.

Global markets are also approaching the point at which larger Middle East production stoppages become unavoidable, as inventory tanks fill and storage capacity is exhausted. Production in the region is already down by an estimated 10 million barrels per day. Industry estimates based on satellite data suggest that the Middle East had around 150 million to 300 million barrels of available tank capacity. At a pace of roughly 10 million barrels per day of stranded production, that amount of storage provides only two to three weeks before additional production must be shut down.

Implications of lower global energy production: recessionary. Some producers have been able to divert limited energy supplies through existing pipeline capacity. However, the industry is running out of time. And once production is shut down, restarting it is not as easy as flipping a switch – it can take weeks, if not months, to come back online. Increased production from outside the Middle East will take time to come online – with non-Middle East producers likely needing assurances that global oil prices will remain above the marginal cost to produce before embarking on additional investment in oil production capacity. In the U.S., the current domestic price of oil, as of this writing – specifically the one-year forward WTI price around $70/bbl – is unlikely to be enough compensation for shale producers to meaningfully increase production given the highly uncertain conditions.

Finally, monetary and fiscal policy have limited ability to respond to a sustained energy shock. Monetary policy would likely be constrained – at least initially – by higher inflation, limiting central banks’ ability to respond to weaker activity and potentially higher unemployment. Central banks globally have already emphasized their commitment to keeping inflation expectations anchored. Fiscal policies that support energy demand, such as gasoline price caps, may do more harm than good. In a scenario involving a 20% disruption in global oil production, prices must rise enough to reduce global demand by 20%. Regional policies that blunt the price mechanism would only increase the price of global oil needed to balance the market.

Bottom line?

While the outlook remains highly uncertain, each passing week increases the global economic costs of the Iran conflict. At some point, potentially soon, as buffers are absorbed, the economic effects of persistent disruptions will start to build, with recessionary implications for the global economy. Energy shortages affecting Asian manufacturing could ripple through global supply chains, leading to broader product shortages and increasing costs. Policies short of de-escalation will have limited effectiveness, and financial conditions could tighten significantly. While markets appear to be betting on a temporary disruption with resolution coming soon, the risk is that they may soon have to contend with the possibility of a more prolonged conflict with greater economic costs.

All investments contain risk and may lose value.

There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. data.title: Hims & Hers' Valuations Back To Earth - Robust Monetization Trends Deserve Reiterated Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS) in January 2026, discussing why I had cautiously upgraded the stock as a speculative Buy then, given the improved risk/reward profile after the prior selloff and the successful monetization of their personalized consumer health offerings.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the HIMS stock, thanks to the much needed bounce off the February 2026 bottom of $13s after the steep selloff from October 2025 highs.

This is especially since their D2C platform continues to report excellent subscriber acquisition trends, growing monetization of their existing subscriber base, and renewed growth prospects from their ongoing expansion into the EU/Canada, with it contributing to the management's reiterated 2030 growth targets.

HIMS Offers Improved Risk/Reward Here

Since my last Buy rating upon the materialization of a trading floor, the bears have proven my thesis wrong, as HIMS lost -44% of its value against the wider market at -4.9% and a similar correction also observed in its peers in varying degrees.

This is albeit with the materialization of a much needed trading floor at the $13s in February 2026, with HIMS' previously oversold technical indicators across the RSIs and trading volumes implying that the worst of the selloff may already be behind us.

1. Legal Clarity & Robust Subscription Trends

This is especially since HIMS' prior lawsuit with Novo Nordisk (NVO) has already been resolved, with it effectively lifting the legal overhang from the former's compounded offerings while opening up a path for a distribution partnership/Wegovy subscription program.

Readers must note that HIMS is still allowed to offer compounded version of GLP-1 offerings, albeit "only for those with a medical necessity as recommended by a doctor."

This development naturally allows the company to continue offering a well diversified, subscription-based consumer health offerings across sexual health, skincare, mental health, hair care, and weight loss care, amongst others.

The success of HIMS' D2C offering has also been exemplified in the robust FQ4'25 performance metrics across:

  • the growing subscriber base to 2.51M (+13% YoY) and
  • the higher Monthly Revenue per Average Subscriber of $83 (+10.6% YoY),

with it underscoring the robust demand for its personalized consumer health offerings and the growing cross-selling trends of its existing customer base, as similarly observed in the expanding total sales share at 47.3% in 2024, pg 18 (+11 points YoY).

These naturally explain the company's outsized revenue growth to $617.81M in FQ4'25 (+28.4% YoY), with the temporarily impacted operating leverage at adj EBITDA margins of 10.7% (-0.5 points YoY) merely attributed to their growing headcount to 2,442 in FY2025 (+49.1% YoY) to support the ongoing geographical footprint expansion.

2. Geographical Expansion To Mitigate Decelerating US Growth

I am of the opinion that HIMS' attempt to drive renewed growth through the geographical expansion has been highly strategic indeed, with the prior acquisitions allowing them to enter new markets beyond the US, namely, Canada and the EU.

The same has been observed in the outsized International revenue growth to $63.67M in FQ4'25 (+825% YoY) against the notably decelerating US revenue growth by +16.8% YoY (compared to +95% YoY a year ago), with it lending credence to the management's promising guidance of "our international footprint to become a more meaningful portion of our revenue in the future."

On the other hand, there is no denying that HIMS has offered a mixed FY2026 revenue guidance of $2.8B at the midpoint (+19.6% YoY) and adj EBITDA margin of 12% at the midpoint (-1.5 points YoY), with it underscoring a notable deceleration from the FY2025 outperformance at +59.1% YoY and 13.5% (+1.6 points YoY), respectively.

These numbers are notably diverging from the management's reiterated FY2030 revenue guidance of at least $6.5B (expanding at a 5Y CAGR of +22.6%) and FY2030 adj EBITDA margin guidance of 20% in the August 2025 Investor Day (expanding from FY2025 levels of 13.5% and FY2019 levels of -80%).

When compared to the historical top-line growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +73.6% and the adj EBITDA expansion at a 2Y CAGR of +153.4% (the period of time that HIMS is adj EBITDA profitable), it is apparent that HIMS' profitable growth profile is already maturing within the US, which perhaps explain their ongoing expansion to new markets.

Pending further growth acceleration and/or the management's raised forward guidance, I can understand why the consensus have already lowered their forward estimates, with HIMS expected to report a decelerating top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +15.6%/+20.8% through FY2028, with it perhaps explaining why the stock has suffered the steep selloff from the 52 week highs.

As a result, I urge readers to closely monitor their execution, since the stock may continue to underperform if its growth profile underwhelm historical trends/2030 guidance, given the premium baked into its valuations (to be further discussed in the next segment).

This is especially since it is uncertain if the NVO partnership announced in March 2026 has been priced into the management's prior FY2026 guidance offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call in February 2026.

HIMS Remains A Buy For The Contrarian

For now, HIMS has lost nearly all of its 2024/2025 stock price gains, albeit finally finding a trading floor at the $13s during the worst of the February 2026 selloff, with it lending credibility to the uptrend support line established since May 2022.

This is especially since HIMS remains expensive at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 16.23x and FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 40.27x, against the sector median of 11.28x/22.21x, respectively, despite the -71.8% stock price correction from the 52 week highs.

It is apparent that the consensus' downgraded forward estimates have presented headwinds to its profitable growth investment thesis, as similarly observed in the mixed, estimated 3Y PEG GAAP ratio of 1.25x, based on the FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 40.27x and the consensus EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +32.1%, against the sector TTM median of 0.91x.

When comparing HIMS' FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 16.23x and FY2025 adj EBITDA margins of 14% (+2 points YoY) to its peers, including:

  • primary care/pharmacy/insurance - CVS Health Corporation (CVS) at 8.87x/4.7% in FY2025,
  • telehealth - LifeMD (LFMD) at 10.60x/7.8% in FY2025,
  • telehealth - Teladoc Health, Inc. (TDOC) at 4.36x/11.1% in FY2025,
  • weight management products/services - WeightWatchers (WW) at 4.08x/18.8% on YTD basis,
  • GLP-1 - Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) at 21.03x/43.4% in FY2025, and
  • GLP-1 - NVO at 8.26x/48.4% in FY2025, respectively,

it is apparent that HIMS is still relatively expensive here, with the ongoing selloff negated by the mixed growth prospects.

Otherwise, based on the current Enterprise Value of $5.07B, the management's FY2030 revenue guidance of at least $6.5B, and the management's FY2030 adj EBITDA guidance of $1.3B, I am of the opinion that the selloff has already unlocked an excellent dip buying opportunity, given the cheap FY2030 EV/Sales valuation of 0.78x and the FY2030 EV/EBITDA valuation of 5x.

This is especially since HIMS still boast a healthy balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 1.24x in FY2025, compared to TDOC at 0.76x, WW at 2.33x, and CVS at 2.73x, aside from LFMD at -2.40x (given the higher cash than debt).

This development implies that the company remains well capitalized to drive further growth opportunities through M&A activities and capacity/fulfillment/geographical expansions to better serve their global customer base/growing subscription trends.

Combined with the previously oversold technical indicators and the excellent insight offered by the uptrend support line established since the May 2022 bottom, I am cautiously reiterating my Buy rating for the HIMS stock here.

This is albeit with the caveat that the stock is only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite, attributed to the potential volatility arising from the still elevated short interest ratio of 37.9% by the time of writing and the beta coefficient of 2.61x.

data.title: Freehold Royalties: An Easy Way To Gain Exposure To Oil Prices
data.description:
Introduction

I picked up a position in Freehold Royalties (FRU:CA) (FRHLF) during COVID and am still holding a portion of that position. I like royalty companies, as they don’t have to deal with spending the sustaining capex to keep existing operations humming along nicely. Once a royalty has been acquired, the royalty owner traditionally does not have to invest any additional capital.

As it had been a while since I last looked at Freehold Royalties, I thought the current high oil price environment offered an excellent opportunity to have another look at this oil and gas royalty company.

Don’t read too much into the Q4 results

The problem with financial reporting is that it is always a backward looking approach. In the case of Freehold, one likely wouldn’t be too inclined to invest in the company if an opinion or investment thesis would be based on the company’s 2025 results. But it goes without saying that every day the WTI oil price remains around $100/barrel, Freehold’s operating cash flow (and free cash flow) will surprise to the upside.

During 2025, Freehold Royalties reported an attributable production of approximately 16,300 barrels of oil-equivalent per day, with approximately 55% of the barrels coming from its Canadian royalties and 45% of the barrels related to its U.S. assets.

As you can see above, the majority of the production consists of liquids, but the 34% in natural gas weighs on the results, as the natural gas price has been pretty weak lately. The average realized price for natural gas during 2025 was just C$1.78 per mcf, and although this represents a 43% increase compared to the previous year, it still is a pretty weak price. Although natural gas sales represented approximately 34% of the oil-equivalent output, it represented just around 7% of the revenue.

As the cash flow statement below shows, the company generated just under C$235 million in operating cash flow. This is based on the aforementioned natural gas prices, as well as an average realized oil price of C$83/barrel. That average realized oil price is based on an average WTI oil price of just under US$65/barrel, but adjusted for the oil quality and applying the differential for the heavy oil that’s part of Freehold’s production mix.

There are no capital expenditures involved with a royalty company (and the only investment-related cash outflows are related to the acquisition of new (royalty or data set) assets. After deducting the very minimal lease expenses, the adjusted FFO was approximately C$234 million, which represents C$1.43 per share, using the current share count of approximately 164 million shares.

This, of course, means the current dividend payments of C$0.09 per share per month remained well-covered, as the C$1.08 in annualized dividends required the company to spend approximately C$177 million on the cash dividends.

Expectations for 2026

Freehold has also provided the market with its guidance for 2026. The company expects to see an attributable production rate of 15,500-16,300 barrels of oil-equivalent per day, with approximately 2/3 of that oil-equivalent output actually consisting of oil and NGLs (with the majority of the liquids production consisting of light oil with some heavy oil and NGLs included in the mix).

Freehold’s guidance also implies the Q1 results may be somewhat disappointing, as the low Canadian natural gas prices in combination with winter storms in the USA have impacted the operational side of things. So while the company is putting forward an average production rhythm, Freehold is guiding for a relatively weak first semester before volumes start to ramp up in the second half of the year.

But this is where it gets interesting. The 2025 results were based on an average oil price of just under US$65/barrel on a WTI basis. Considering the production of light and medium oil is approximately 6,700 barrels per day (and I don’t anticipate seeing any major changes in 2026), applying an oil price of US$80/barrel would boost the cash flow by US$100,000 per day. That’s US$36.5 million per week, and after taking the taxes into account and applying the current USD/CAD exchange rate, the FCF per share could increase by approximately C$0.25, and likely even more, as a higher WTI price and stable WCS oil price differentials will also have a positive impact on the realized price of heavy oil (which represents just over 1,500 barrels per day) and add a few cents to the FFO/share as well.

Investment thesis

I have a long position in Freehold Royalties, as I consider royalty companies to be a lower-risk vehicle to gain exposure to the oil price. I had hoped for the Freehold share price to have been a bit stronger given the current spot price for oil, but the market appears to be a bit hesitant. That being said, based on my calculations, the stock is currently trading at approximately 9.5-10 times the free cash flow using $80 WTI in a base case scenario.

I’m firmly holding onto my stock. Although we have only seen a higher oil price for just a month, every day WTI trades close to $100/barrel will boost the cash flow and rapidly reduce the net debt (which stood at C$282 million at the end of last year). At an $80 WTI oil price throughout the year, Freehold Royalties should generate in excess of C$100 million in free cash flow after completing the dividend payments.

data.title: EGGY: Explaining Its Ins And Outs
data.description:
This article covers NestYield Dynamic Income ETF (EGGY) as part of Seeking Alpha's ETF educational series. Methodologically, the article assesses EGGY from a neutral perspective where I communicate its mandate, key drivers, nuances as well as matters related to a market participant's portfolio suitability and risk expectations.

Headline Methodology

EGGY uses factor-based decision-making to invest in a portfolio of U.S. equities. In addition, EGGY overlays its equities portfolio with options strategies such as a covered calls program, tail risk hedging, and standalone bets on index and individual equities. It's mandate is flexible and it doesn't follow a clear rules-based thematic system. Here are some of its operational features:

Direct and Indirect Equities Exposure

EGGY's equity selection process includes a screening model that filters stocks with criteria such as market capitalization, market share, projected revenue growth, earnings per share growth, price to equity ratio, profit margin, and capital expenditures. The fund's analysts will typically draw equities from its screener, proceed to qualitative analysis and select between 10-25 stocks, which EGGY either owns directly or through options. The following diagram shows EGGY's exposure as-per 01/04/2026 – notice the lean direct ownership in issuers.

Treasuries

The fund holds treasuries for collateral and additional income. Limited information is provided regarding the total return and risk attribution bound to Treasuries.

Options for Tail Risk Hedging

EGGY uses put options on indices and individual issuers to protect against material downside risk. It openly admits that the cost of put options can drag on upside gains. As such, its mandate provides freedom to execute spread-based strategies as cost-mitigating tools. For example, it will deploy credit or debt spread hedges (broadly explained via this link) to manage costs and margin requirements.

Covered Call

EGGY uses numerous options strategies to generate income, like covered calls. Covered call strategies consist of owning the underlying asset while simultaneously selling a call option, with the aim that the premium received from writing the option creates a net gain despite downside movement in the stock price. Note that upwards momentum past the call's strike price would be sacrificed gains. The following diagram shown an example of a covered call's payoff structure.

Collectively, the fund has delivered a total return of 10.89% since its inception in December 2024 while charging an expense ratio of 0.95%. However, notably, the fund has shed value, shown by its -23.11% price drawdown. The culprits for its NAV erosion is likely return of capital (discussion to follow) and underperformance in income-generating options strategies (figure 2).

Let's dial in on EGGY's distribution. As previously mentioned, the fund has recently underperformed on net investment income and delivered return of capital, which is shown by figure 2. Having reviewed EGGY's prospectus, I gathered a sense of elasticity in distribution policy. For example, the company states: "The Board has adopted a Distribution (Rule 12b-1) Plan (the “Plan”) pursuant to Rule 12b-1 under the 1940 Act. In accordance with the Plan, the Funds are authorized to pay an amount up to 0.25% of its average daily net assets each year to pay distribution fees for the sale and distribution of its Shares." However, other parts of the prospectus merely point to income and capital being distributed on a discretionary basis with an option of re-investment. In my opinion, we'll see the fund distribute what it can. If its annual gains/income fall short, we might still see it distribute through return of capital. If the latter occurs, taxes are important to take note of (discussed in a later sub-section).

Side Note: EGGY has distributed on a monthly basis since its inception. It's claimed that income distributions will occur monthly and capital gains distributions "at least annually."

Key Drivers

The Proprietary Strategy's Effectiveness

EGGY's investors rely on a proprietary strategy. The fund has designed its own stock screening method, which is has overlaid with a discretionary options program. Although EGGY might arguably deliver substantial success via its decision-making framework, investors' returns are bound to the success of internal decisions and not just the market's general direction.

Regime Detection

Management's ability to detect volatility and stock market regimes will be salient to EGGY's performance. The fund's prospectus repeatedly alludes to phenomenon such as gaining exposure to assets that it determines over/underprices or detecting whether markets are in a stable condition or not; as a result, we'll likely see EGGY being driven by the following.

Underlying Assets

The success of EGGY's stock selection hinges on general market direction and the idiosyncratic performance of its concentrated portfolio. EGGY's ability to detect macro, sectoral, and style regimes is incredibly important, given its active approach.

Income-based options strategies

It's critical to note that option premiums generally expand during periods of higher market volatility, which could either improve EGGY's option-writing profitability or increase pure hedging cost. In addition, equity market directionality and volatility have a negative relationship, which influences EGGY's directional exposure and its covered call strategies (covered calls rely on stale markets). As a result, we can fairly say that volatility regime detection is paramount to EGGY's success. While regimes can be detected, volatility is subject to randomness, making it a guessing game at times.

Portfolio Suitability

Alternative Exposure

EGGY provides diversified exposure if compared to the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), SPDR Gold Shares (GLD), and the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND). I expect the ETF to maintain its diversification due to its implementation of options strategies. Whether it out or underperforms remains uncertain. Nevertheless, an independent source of return is evident.

Income-Seeking Exposure

The fund aims to generate income at a target monthly level of 1.5-3% of its net asset value. I am unsure whether the target is realistic, but I'll leave that for you to decide. What I can confirm is that EGGY has distributed on a regular basis and that it maintained an average yield of 12.22% in 2025 (its first full trading year). Equity markets soared last year, leading the ETF into a capital gain per share of $5.40 and total income from investment operations of $5.24 per share. Despite that, EGGY subsidized its total distribution per share of $9.04 with $7.78 in return of capital (~86%). In essence, the early signs are: the fund will likely deliver solid income to investors throughout its tenure with the drawback of net asset value erosion.

Additional Risks

The Multipart Mandate

EGGY's mandate is slightly split between objectives. Its theme is essentially to maintain long exposure via bottom-up equities strategy while overlaying a range of options strategies to minimize drawdown while maximizing upside, making it difficult to determine what the vehicle's expected return could be.

Value-at-Risk Is Real

The ETF's prospectus suggests options strategies can mitigate downside risk. Although somewhat exaggerated by EGGY's distribution policy and a short timeframe, a look-back shows that EGGY's 5% Value-at-Risk and maximum drawdown metrics are both similar to those of the S&P 500. In fact, the ETF's monthly rolling value-at-risk of 7.38% is higher than the S&P 500's 4.75%.

Side Note: A monthly Value-at-Risk of 5% reveals the loss suffered at the 95th percentile during the past 12-months.

Taxation Planning

EGGY distributed a large sum of return of capital in 2025. It is also mandated to distribute return on capital and income. The broad distribution strategy can make tax planning difficult. For example, return of capital will be taxed as a capital gain because it reduces an investor's cost basis. However, in contrast, the distribution of investment income will be taxed as an ordinary dividend.

Conclusion

The NestYield Dynamic Income ETF derives its returns from an underlying basket of equities and various options strategies.

Its directional exposure includes a quantitative screening process and qualitative analysis, whereby exposure is gained through direct and indirect holdings of individual issuers. The options program is multi-functional. For example, EGGY operates a covered call strategy for income and uses spread-based hedges to mitigate drawdown risk.

Among the vehicle's key drivers are managerial skill, volatility regime detection, and market regime awareness. As a result of its firm distribution policy, EGGY opts for a blended distribution approach by using return of capital, income distributions, and return on investment distributions. Although its structure is flexible, its mandate lacks clarity and its Value-at-Risk is similar to the S&P 500's, posing key risks.

While acquired by various types of investors, EGGY is generally suitable to portfolios with income or diversified return needs. Despite its carefully-planned mandate, clear understanding aught to be garnered before engaging.

This article answers three main questions about EGGY:

  1. What are EGGY's objectives and how does it aim to achieve them?
  2. What risks and benefits accompany EGGY's strategy?
  3. What purpose would EGGY have in a portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: TDVI: The Best Way For Income Investors To Invest In Tech
data.description:
Income investors have historically been at a huge disadvantage as the stock market has increasingly shifted toward the technology sector.

Fast-growing tech stocks notoriously pay out very little in dividends. Many pay no dividends at all. The ones that do pay dividends are typically older and more mature companies growing slower than their smaller, newer, high-tech peers.

Some income investors have chosen to forsake their financial goal of maximizing current income in order to enjoy the price appreciation from the technology sector, while others have stuck to their guns and felt left out as tech has crushed the rest of the market, including their value-oriented dividend payers.

Just look at the difference in total returns between value and growth over the last 20 years:

For this chart, I used the State Street Technology Sector ETF (XLK) and Invesco Nasdaq Index (QQQ) to represent techy growth and the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) and iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) to represent value.

The level of outperformance, which ramped up after the Great Financial Crisis and continued ever since, is astounding.

Unlike the manufacturing-driven economy of the mid-20th century, the US economy has become increasingly technology-driven.

That only becomes truer as population growth grinds toward zero, causing the economic weight to shift from the size of the labor force to productivity.

Little to no growth in the working-age population in developed countries, including the US, necessitates heavy investment into productivity enhancements.

As growth in the working-age population slows and reverses all across the developed world, productivity-enhancing technology has never been so important.

Put simply, today's income investor can't afford to sit out from tech.

That's where covered call ETFs focused on the technology sector come in. Several have been launched over the last five years or so.

The best of them, in my opinion, is the FT Vest Technology Target Income ETF (TDVI), which currently yields about 8%.

Let's dig in to this interesting tech-driven income opportunity.

The Case For TDVI

Okay, where should we start? The beginning, I suppose.

TDVI is a covered call ETF; it owns a portfolio of common stocks, but it actually writes call options on either the Nasdaq Index or the S&P 500 (or both) to generate ongoing income. Specifically, it writes at-the-money calls in order to maximize premium income. If these indices rise quickly, then strike prices will be hit, thereby capping the stock portfolio's upside.

It's almost like owning two funds: a passive dividend-tech portfolio (stock selection tracking the Nasdaq Technology Dividend Index) as well as an active buy-write strategy.

When the stock market is rangebound or slowly rising, the fund generates high premium income without hitting strike prices, which is pretty much the sweet spot for these funds. But when the stock market is ripping higher and strike prices are being hit, the covered call ETF's price appreciation is stunted.

TDVI targets a yield about 8 percentage points above the Nasdaq's dividend yield, which typically sits under 1%. Theoretically, then, TDVI should continuously yield 8% or more.

In compensation for participation in this strategy, First Trust and its sub-advisory Vest Financial charge an expense ratio of 0.75% for TDVI.

That's fairly high compared to TDVI's direct peers, but as we'll see below, the ETF has more than earned its high fees so far.

What exactly does TDVI's equity portfolio consist of?

Enter stage left: The First Trust NASDAQ Technology Dividend Fund (TDIV), a Morningstar 4-star rated ETF focused solely on dividend-paying companies in the technology and communication services sectors.

This passively constructed ETF has 95 holdings and sports a 30-day SEC dividend yield of 1.65%. It aims for 80% of its portfolio to be technology stocks and 20% to be communications stocks. Basically, to be eligible for inclusion, a company in either of these sectors has to have paid a regular dividend over the last 12 months, be at least $500 million in market cap, and yield at least 0.5%.

Holdings are weighted by total dividend payout, which ends up privileging larger companies and those with a higher payout ratio.

It should be acknowledged how high-quality this list is.

International Business Machines Corporation (IBM), Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), and Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) all boast A-level credit ratings.

The risk of dividend cuts at these companies is extremely low. The question, instead, is what level of earnings and dividend growth should be expected.

As I've pointed out in my weekend articles, while the profitability of the hyperscalers' heavy AI infrastructure investment is uncertain, the tech hardware companies should continue to benefit from this CapEx for a long time to come.

That makes it encouraging to see hardware (semiconductors plus various equipment) making up roughly half of TDIV's portfolio:

The other half is in software and various tech-related services.

Here's an even simpler breakdown of the portfolio:

I like this roughly 50/50 split between hardware and software.

While the market is currently panicking about the potential for AI to disrupt software-as-a-service business models, I think that at least the more mature and established software companies with sufficient recurring cash flows to pay dividends should remain unharmed or even benefit from AI. After all, there are other competitive advantages in the software industry besides just the ability to cheaply write code.

And, as indicated above, I think many tech hardware businesses are direct beneficiaries of the massive amount of AI infrastructure CapEx being carried out.

So, in short, the portfolio looks strong and well-positioned for growth.

Also, at a forward P/E ratio of about 21x, TDIV is a bit cheaper than QQQ's ~23x and XLK's 23.5x multiples.

But how does TDIV perform against these other tech growth ETFs?

Well, since its inception in August 2012, TDIV has underperformed.

However (and this is a very important "however"), literally 100% of that underperformance occurred from 2012 through 2021.

Since the beginning of 2022, TDIV has perfectly kept pace with its techy growth ETF peers.

Here's TDIV compared to QQQ and XLK over the last three years, which we might think of as the "AI Era":

Why exactly TDIV suddenly began to keep pace with QQQ and XLK at the beginning of 2022 is an interesting question that I can't confidently answer.

My guess would be that the best performing technology and communications companies during the 2010s and early 2020s paid zero dividends and were thus ineligible for inclusion in TDIV's portfolio.

Or maybe it has something to do with the ramp-up in Big Tech capex over the last several years, benefitting TDIV's hardware holdings.

Whatever the cause, the performance gap between TDIV and its broader techy growth ETF peers has been eliminated.

And with tech giants like Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), (GOOGL) recently initiating dividends and beginning to grow them, more of Big Tech will soon be eligible for inclusion in TDIV's portfolio.

You might be thinking that I've hardly discussed TDVI for a while.

But TDVI's portfolio is 100% the same as that of TDIV, and therefore understanding TDIV's performance is a critical prerequisite to understanding TDVI's performance.

Let's now compare TDVI's total returns (price movement + dividends) to that of its covered call ETF peers like the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) and JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ):

As you can see, since inception, TDVI has meaningfully outperformed both of these peers.

In fact, I am not aware of any tech-focused covered call ETF that has outperformed TDVI in total returns. If you know of one, please let me know in the comments.

Recall also that TDVI has much higher fees than these two peers -- over double the expense ratio, in fact. That is why I say that TDVI has so far more than earned its high expense ratio of 0.75%.

The combination of TDIV's conservative-tech dividend growers and Vest Financial's covered call strategy seems to be working well.

Buy The Dip

As of this writing, TDIV and TDVI are rebounding on optimism about de-escalation in the Middle East. But they remain in a buyable dip, in my view.

The technology sector remains the fastest growing part of the stock market, by far.

This astounding earnings growth is largely drive by massive AI infrastructure investments being made by the hyperscalers as well as some third-party data center developers/landlords.

Hundreds of billions of dollars are flowing into the construction and outfitting of data centers, which will inevitably benefit all types of technology companies.

And yet, despite this phenomenal growth based on a generational growth trend, the technology sector trades at roughly the same forward P/E ratio as the broader market.

Unless the analyst consensus for earnings growth is wrong, this is simply a mispricing. Tech deserves a higher multiple than the S&P 500.

That applies also to TDIV's dividend-paying tech and communications stocks.

Morningstar shows that TDIV's long-term earnings growth forecast (next 3 years) is about 11.3% annually. Dividend payers are more mature and slower growing but also typically more stable and conservative.

Bottom Line

I have personally been buying both TDIV and TDVI recently. You can trust that this chef eats his own cooking.

I truly believe that no investor — young or old, working or retired, appreciation-seeking or income-seeking — can afford to avoid technology in the world of low population growth we are entering.

In the past, I haven't prioritized technology. I didn't buy the dip in 2022 and regretted it. I don't want to make that mistake again this time.

For income-seeking investors who feel the same, I believe TDVI is your best way to gain exposure to technology while generating high, monthly paid dividends. Vest Financial has historically done a great job of actively managing its buy-write strategy to generate a high yield while maintaining some price appreciation.

But for younger investors like myself, I think TDIV is the better choice for long-term total returns and dividend growth.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Snowflake: Agentic AI And Data Cloud Winner - Upgrade To Speculative Buy
data.description:
I previously reiterated my Hold rating for Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW) in September 2025, given the risks from the overstretched valuations/technical indicators notably negating its renewed SaaS growth prospects.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am finally upgrading the SNOW stock as a Speculative Buy after numerous Hold/Sell ratings, given the improved risk/reward after the recent SaaS meltdown and the emergence of a technical bottoming.

This is especially since the AI SaaS company has continued to prove its relevance through accretive M&A activities and in-house R&D efforts, all of which underscoring their relevance during the ongoing Agentic AI and Data Cloud boom.

SNOW's Agentic AI & Data Cloud Story Looks More Convincing Here

Since then, my caution surrounding SNOW's potentially painful correction has indeed materialized as previously posited, with the stock experiencing a steep SaaSpocalypse pullback along the wider SaaS sector.

This development lent credence to my prior warning surrounding the risks of chasing overpriced stocks at a time of AI exuberance, since it was apparent that its valuations had ran away from its fundamentals then.

Otherwise, there is no denying SNOW's robust AI-driven growth prospects, with the integration of their core AI Data Cloud offering across their new capabilities already streamlining their customers' data life cycle across acquisition, analysis, interpretation, and production while delivering accelerated productivity, including:

  • Snowflake Intelligence (Generative/Agentic AI analytical capabilities),
  • Cortex Code (AI coding agent for agent/app building and productivity workflows), and
  • strategic model partnership with numerous LLM leaders, including Google (GOOG), OpenAI, and Anthropic.

Without regurgitating SNOW's FQ4'26 numbers, it is apparent that the higher "telemetry activity on the platform" (automated data monitoring/analysis) have triggered the customers' increased consumption trends and the consequently growing product revenues - all of which underscoring their Agentic AI beneficiary status.

Readers must not forget the tailwinds from their excellent go-to-market strategy and the focus on larger Enterprise deals on their performance metrics, with it already contributing to the outsized multi-year Remaining Performance Obligation's [RPO] expansion to $9.77B in FQ4'26 (+23.9% QoQ/+42.4% YoY).

This is on top of the tailwinds from SNOW's recent acquisitions:

  • Observe - allowing them to offer improved data monitoring outcome and operational resilience,
  • Datometry Migration Solution - allowing them to migrate legacy data to SNOW's data warehouse at "lower cost and with minimal disruption" and naturally, reduce friction/acquire new customers easily, along with
  • Select Star - allowing them to expand their in-house data catalog through Select Star's embedded metadata and data discovery capability,

with it underscoring their focus on delivering renewed growth and expanded data capabilities while effectively putting their prior FQ4'24 revenue growth headwinds (arising from the introduction of the tiered storage pricing and the increased adoption of the open-source Iceberg Table formats) behind them.

These reasons are also why SNOW has been able to report robust adoption/cross selling trends and the rich Net Revenue Retention Rate of 125% (-1 points YoY).

This is while defying the recent sector pessimism surrounding the SaaS monetization trends, as observed in:

  • the higher net add for the Customers Over $1M Product Revenue at 157 (compared to a year ago at 125) and
  • the total customers at 2.33K (compared to a year ago 1.18K),

significantly aided by their consumption-based pricing model against that of the per-seat model.

This is especially since SNOW has walked the talk on AI delivering improved productivities, as they hint at an improved framework "for investing in growth. It's no longer tied to headcount," with it implying their ability to drive further operating leverage/efficiencies moving forward.

The same has been observed in their expanding adj operating income to $139.17M in FQ4'26 (+49.9% YoY) and adj operating margins to 11% (+2 points YoY), with FY2027 likely to bring forth richer bottom-line performance if the management sustains their current framework.

These reasons may also be why SNOW has felt confident to offer a promising FY2027 guidance across:

  • revenue growth to $5.66B (+26.6% YoY),
  • adj operating margins of 12.5% (+2.5 points YoY), and
  • adj Free Cash Flow margins of 23% (-2 points YoY),

with it building upon the robust FY2026 performance at +29.1% YoY, 10% (+4 points YoY), and 25% (-1 points YoY), respectively.

The consensus have notably raised their forward estimates, with SNOW expected to report an excellent top-line growth at a CAGR of +24.5% through FY2029, with the acceleration against 'the expanding AI Data Cloud Total Addressable Market at 5Y CAGR of +15.8% to $335B in CY2029' implying their tailwinds from the ongoing Agentic AI boom.

The same has been observed in the projected acceleration in their adj EPS at a CAGR of +39.4% through FY2029 and the management's projection of SBC expenses to decline from FY2025 revenue ratio of 41% to FY2027 ratio of ~27%, with it lending credibility to their slower headcount growth and AI-driven operating efficiencies over the next few years.

Focus On EV/Sales, Pending Bottom-Line Acceleration

On the one hand, despite the raised consensus forward estimates and the -46.2% stock price correction from the 52 week highs, it is apparent that SNOW remains expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 84.21x, despite the notable moderation from the 4Y mean of 324.87x (the period of time that they are adj EPS profitable).

A similar conclusion may also be derived against my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.13x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 84.21x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +39.4%, compared to the 4Y mean of 1.52x and the sector median of 1.27x.

On the other hand, part of SNOW's premium valuations may be attributed to its FY2026 Rule of 54% outperformance (-2 points YoY), based on the FY2026 revenue growth by +29% YoY (compared to a year ago at +30% YoY) and FY2026 FCF margins of 25% (compared to a year ago at 26%).

When comparing SNOW's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 8.46x and the FY2026 Rule of 54% outperformance to its diversified AI SaaS peers, including:

  • Enterprise/hyperscaler - Oracle (ORCL) at 7.86x/-41.1% (LTM revenue growth by +14.9% YoY and FCF margins of -56%),
  • Enterprise/hyperscaler - Microsoft (MSFT) at 8.23x/42% (LTM revenue growth by +16.7% sequentially and FCF margins of 25.3%),
  • Customer Relationship Management - Salesforce (CRM) at 3.91x/44% (FY2026 revenue growth by +10% YoY and FCF margins of 34%), and
  • Enterprise/Federal analytics focus - Palantir (PLTR) at 44.33x/107% (FY2025 revenue growth by +56% YoY and FCF margins of 51%), respectively,

it is apparent that SNOW profitable growth trends (on a revenue and FCF basis) has been well regarded by the market, with it contributing to the still premium stock price valuations despite the recent selloff.

This is pending the multi-year improvement in its operating leverage on an adj operating margin and/or adj EPS basis.

If anything, SNOW has been putting much of their FCF generation to good use, with the $873M of shares repurchased in FY2026 (-54.9% YoY from a year ago at $1.93B) balancing the dilutive impact of its elevated SBC expenses, as observed in the somewhat reasonable shares outstanding expansion to 342.29M (+3.2% YoY).

Combined with the still healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $1.75B (-25.5% YoY), I am of the opinion that the management has been highly competent in their capital allocation across M&A growth opportunities and shareholder return thus far.

SNOW Is Cautiously Upgraded As A Speculative Buy

For now, SNOW has already returned much of their 2025 gains, with it remaining to be seen if the bulls may defend the $150s after bouncing off those levels twice in February 2026.

I am of the opinion that the worst of SNOW's correction may already be over, aided by the oversold indicators across the RSIs/trading volumes.

This is especially since the management's FY2027 revenue growth outlook by +27% YoY (compared to a year ago at +29% YoY) and FY2027 FCF margin outlook of 23% (-2 points YoY) imply at a robust FY2027 Rule of 50% performance (-4 points YoY), with further expansion likely based on the three beat and raise performances between FQ1'26-FQ3'26.

Combined with the potential bottoming at current levels, I am cautiously upgrading the SNOW stock as a Speculative Buy here.

This is albeit with the caveat that the AI SaaS sector is likely to underperform in the near term, attributed to the rapidly developing macroeconomic/geopolitical environment triggering the pessimistic stock market sentiments and the uncertain market rotation from the current emphasis on value stocks to Big Tech/AI/growth stocks.

This implies that SNOW may very well continue consolidating at these levels pending that recovery catalyst, with the stock only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory.

data.title: Massive March Jobs Beat: Stock Futures And Bonds Trade Lower On Good Friday
data.description:
The March jobs report was much better than expected. The 178,000 payrolls rise was above even the highest economist’s estimate and was the best gain since December 2024. Of course, there may have been some positive payback from weak early-year labor market trends driven by one-off factors.

The three-month average number of jobs created is now 68,000 (above the perceived breakeven level required to keep the unemployment rate steady). Just 7,000 jobs were taken off from January and February, softer than the trend from the past eight months.

The unemployment rate fell sharply from 4.441% in February to 4.256% in March. The U-6 “underemployment” rate, however, rose to 8.0%. Average hourly earnings were particularly weak, up just 0.2% in March from February, bringing the year-over-year rate to just 3.5%--the lowest since May 2021. Average hours worked slipped to 34.2 per week from 34.3.

Elsewhere, manufacturing employment rose by 15,000, the most since November 2023, and the Health Care sector was responsible for 76,000 of the headline NFP gain. What's more, the government shed 8,000 positions last month. The number of permanent job losers fell to 1.9 million, while the percentage of unemployed workers for more than 27 weeks rose to 25.4% from 25.3% in February. The prime age labor force participation rate inched lower to 83.8% from 83.9%.

In markets, equity futures took their cue from the bond market. Treasury yields rose in electronic trading, putting downward pressure on S&P 500 futures (which closed at 9:15 a.m. ET for Good Friday).

March Unemployment Rate Falls, Big Headline Jobs Gain

Massive beat +178k March jobs... best since December 2024

The economy added an average of 15,000 jobs over the past six months and 68,000 per month so far in 2026

+186k private payrolls... best since December 2024

Very cool: Average hourly earnings just +3.5% YoY... lowest since May 2021

+15k Manufacturing payrolls.. best since November 2023

Fresh multi-month low in the unemployment rate.. 4.256%.. almost rounded down to 4.2%

The unemployment rate ticked down to 4.3%, but the U-6 underemployment rate rose to 8.0%

Equity Futures Fell Following the NFP Data

Treasury Yields Rose After the March Jobs Report

U.S. Recession Risk Has Dropped to 38%

Looking ahead, more key March data hits the tape on Monday with the ISM Services PMI data—be on watch for the key Prices Paid subindex. Durable Goods, a February data point, prints Tuesday morning, followed by an early-year look at Consumer Credit. More market-moving updates begin on Wednesday, highlighted by the minutes from the March FOMC meeting.

Jobless claims and a third look at Q4 GDP keep the macro busy, but Friday’s March CPI report will be the real volatility driver. Finally, a preliminary read on UMich Consumer Sentiment rounds out the week, with bank earnings the following week.

FOMC Minutes, CPI Next Week

The Bottom Line

It was a very strong March jobs report. The 178,000 payrolls gain was well above the 51,000 consensus, while the unemployment rate fell sharply to 4.26%. The market response was weak, as the data leave the Fed in a tough spot with elevated inflation.

data.title: Wheaton Precious Metals: BHP Deal Adds Even More Risk At Elevated Prices
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM), I highlighted their solid but relatively risky exposure to gold and silver, with a leading growth pipeline, strong financials, and a good track record, rating them a Hold since their stock was trading around a fair value for the commodity prices we saw back then.

With the stock up as a result of their strong performance tied to the jump in gold and silver prices, WPM remains a Hold, trading slightly above what I would consider a fair value (same as before), while the company’s overall quality makes it worth paying attention to despite the geographic exposure and broader macro risks.

Foundation Still Shines

WPM’s 2025 was solid overall, with sales nearly doubling from $1.28 billion in 2024 to $2.31 billion in 2025 and net earnings nearly tripling to $1.47 billion, further highlighting the kind of leverage this type of company offers, beating their gold high-end production guidance (alongside the market’s estimates) by ~6.74% and ~22.41% for other metals, with silver production near the top of the expectations, for a beat of ~3.23% on a GEO basis.

We can see a significant jump in FCF as a result of the increase in production and especially commodity prices, with significant investments in mineral stream interests - which more than doubled to $1.34 billion - while the dividend was a very sustainable ~$296.37 million last year and recently got increased by ~18%, for a ~0.57% yield.

WPM’s growth pipeline remains one of the main advantages compared to peers, with several investments made in recent history fueling their near-term growth, expected to expand their GEO (gold equivalent ounce) production by ~50% by 2030. They expect to maintain attributable production at ~1.2 million GEOs, while maintaining their majority gold-silver exposure, which also sets them apart from most other peers.

As a note, especially given the kind of money they and the miners get in the current environment, it’s very likely to see many other opportunities that can help them grow this pipeline beyond 2030.

For 2026, they expect their production to continue growing especially in terms of silver, reaching 860,000 to 940,000 GEOs, with their pipeline advancing towards their long-term goal, with boosts coming from projects such as Blackwater, Mineral Park, Fenix, Hemlo, Goose or Platreef helping offset the fall in Constancia and a slight decrease at their flagship Salobo following a peak year.

Financially, based on WPM’s latest report, they remain in a very strong position that’s common for companies in the industry (especially following the jump in gold and silver prices), with current assets covering their total liabilities, although this is before they closed a major deal that I’ll mention below, which adds a bit of leverage but is still far from an issue from a financial point of view.

The BHP Deal

A few days ago, the company’s CEO was officially replaced, stepping down after more than 15 years of leading the business (will hold a position in the Board to assure the smooth transition). However, he didn’t leave until adding what others and I consider a bad deal to the company’s portfolio - despite his strong track record of building this into an industry powerhouse - the BHP (BHP) deal where they give the miner $4.3 billion upfront and pay 20% of the spot price for a 33.75% silver stream in the major Antamina project in Peru. This will be funded through a mix of cash on hand and debt, as you can see in the image above.

Fellow analyst Trapping Value modeled the numbers behind this transaction in a recent article dedicated to it, and this does look like a terrible deal unless we see a major fundamental jump in silver prices (XAGUSD:CUR) or they assumed a much lower discount rate, which to me looks like buying into significant FOMO at overhyped prices driven by trader sentiment rather than fundamentals, which is a big issue if that’s most of what they do with the significant gains seen from the current bull run.

They announced other deals in the meantime (including its entry into Australia after the deal with KGL), but I’ll also note that the company paid $4.3 billion for the rights to 90% of BHP’s 33.75% of the silver (drops to 22.5% for the remainder of the mine life after 100 million ounces are delivered) after already owning 33.75% tied to their 2015 deal with Glencore (GLCNF) - for which they paid $900 million, further emphasizing what might as well be buying at a terrible time.

Not only did they pay a fraction (~20.9%) for a similar deal back then, but the deal with Glencore is actually superior since the stream drops to 22.5% after 140 Moz, not 100 Moz like this one, and the Glencore one uses a 100% payable rate compared to 90% for the BHP deal.

This is the difference between the kind of deal that made Wheaton what it is today (closed at very low prices when miners needed the money) and what buying into the market’s hype looks like. Sure, if silver is getting a significant repricing, then this deal makes sense and it can be quite profitable, but I believe the company had the kind of leverage, power and size needed to find better ones and not take this kind of risk when commodity prices skyrocketed - especially since they're putting even more eggs in an already large basket since they had a big stream here already.

Potential Long-Term PM Repricing

The main factor behind the recent volatility is the ongoing Iran conflict, and even though it may be counterintuitive to see precious metals falling as a result of a significant macro conflict that can destabilize the global economy, this is in part the result of a flight to safety into the dollar (and potential profit-taking in precious metals).

This pushes the price of these commodities down in dollar terms alongside treasuries, boosting treasury yields and potentially leading to even more demand for the dollar since these notes are now offering more attractive yields that can help shield money away from the potential inflation and fear.

I highlighted before how the recent jump in precious metals was mostly the result of a massive jump in demand coming from investing, while their other sources of demand (e.g. banks, jewelry especially for gold) dropped or was relatively stable, which I believe comes as a double-edged blade. As seen over the past years, a supply deficit was not enough to cause major jumps, as silver for example has been running on a deficit for over half a decade now based on data from the Silver Institute and peers.

This can be a major reason why we see the significant jumps in these commodities over the past months and at least in the near-term, as the more “rational” demand fell while leaving more room to speculative, FOMO, hype, etc. demand, which is not the worst thing since it can open some opportunities such as several I highlighted in gold mining recently.

Again, this is not a matter of phones or other electronics using less silver, for example, but the current macro pressure combined with the fact that higher material prices will mostly be passed to the final consumer to maintain margins can result in people not affording that phone anymore or delaying the acquisition to instead focus on paying rent.

However, this may not last for that long, as I don’t expect the the ongoing Iran conflict to last for a prolonged period (detailed why here), and even though we might see a period of pressure in the near-term due to the inflationary pressure that can delay rate cuts or even lead to hikes - which can further strengthen the dollar - the fundamentals remain in favor of a repricing.

Once this conflict is over and the previous trajectory recovers, the market may change its expectations and the opposite of the current flight to safety into the dollar can revert, while the previous issues (e.g., rising debt, economic/political uncertainty, Japan’s potential treasury selloff, etc.) can drive the narrative again, potentially supporting higher prices going forward, especially after all the money printing.

Meanwhile, silver can also benefit from several long-term trends especially in AI/electrification - unlike gold - which adds to the metal’s potential beyond its precious metal status and could help ensure solid levels of demand.

Risks Reiterated

I’ll highlight once again the geography risks that WPM comes with, as we’re talking about a company with significant exposure to countries like Brazil, Peru or Mexico that the market currently trades at terrible discount rates, which is not reflected in WPM’s price despite the risk being relatively high compared to peers. As a note, Vale’s (VALE) Salobo mine in Brazil represented roughly 37% of the company’s production mix in 2025, and even once we account for all the diversification by 2030, that’s still estimated to represent 26% by that time, while Antamina would represent another 18% combined.

Meanwhile, especially given the new supply-demand dynamics at least in the near-term, it wouldn’t be impossible to see more volatility, and even though that won’t be enough to affect WPM as a company that much unless we’re talking about major drops, the stock can continue to be volatile.

The company’s impressive growth can also be hurt by potential delays, and I also noted in more detail recently how future production is (and should be) discounted by the market, which is another thing that’s not that visible here.

Valuation

For WPM's DCF to Equity, using the same realized prices as in 2025 as a base (for a blended GEO of $3,554/oz) and an average cash cost of $514, let's assume the company's operating margins will be $3,040 throughout the cycle, which assumes lower prices than today. Given their balance sheet, I'll also not account for any changes in net borrowings, to highlight their organic potential.

Based on their production guidance, we would get to a FCF of $2.63 billion in 2026, $2.85 billion in 2027, $3.07 billion in 2028, $3.29 billion in 2029 (assuming that they're adding 75,000 GEO per year from 2026 to 2030) and $3.51 billion in 2030, which I'll assume can grow at a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years from potential macro improvements (above their flat estimate) and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with what I consider an appropriate discount rate of 8% to take into account their great quality but also the level of risk their portfolio comes with, as well as 454.84 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

Note that I’m assuming for a 3% CAGR from potential improvements for 2031-2035 without assuming any investments in mineral stream interests, to be extra generous to account for potential new discoveries or other accretive factors.

Note that a 3% CAGR may not look like much, but over 5 years it's almost the same as adding a new Antamina in terms of production. If we wanted to account for growth CAPEX (investments in stream interests in this case), it should’ve been discounted from the FCF.

This gets us to an equity value of $57.63 billion, for an intrinsic value of $126.71, which is below the current $135.56 at the moment of writing this. This would place their P/FCF multiple at about 18.6x based on the year 10 FCF and the terminal value, which I also consider fair for a company like this, as we're basically not assuming any growth CAPEX (in this case, that would be the investments in mineral interests).

Conclusions

Although they’re trading above my intrinsic fair value estimate that already assumes that generous 3% CAGR despite using a model that theoretically highlights their organic potential from this state onwards (without investments), I’ll rate WPM a Hold, as their quality and higher prices in the future can justify a price like the one we see today.

However, that’s not to say there aren’t risks. The company’s exposure to what the market generally considers very risky countries when it comes to virtually any other investment (look at the P/FCF Vale usually trades at), and the asset concentration after the BHP deal is not getting much better, while the ongoing macro uncertainty and significant speculative demand can add even more levels of volatility and uncertainty.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: March Jobs Report: Significant Bounce Back, Health Care Sector Leads
data.description:
In an otherwise quiet day, with U.S. equity markets closed for the holiday weekend, Friday’s job report provided some good news to investors juggling continuing market volatility.

On the final trading day prior to the holiday, indexes reversed course on two-day momentum following President Trump’s speech on the war with Iran on Wednesday evening.

Following that speech, all three indexes remain in the red on a YTD basis, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), S&P 500 (SPY), and Nasdaq Composite (NDX), all down between 3% and 5%. Yields on long-term yields, meanwhile, continue to track within the 4.35% to 4.4% range.

With the holiday closure, investors will have to wait until Monday to express their reaction to today’s report. In my view, attention likely will have already pivoted back to developments on the war front by then.

Today’s report was a positive bounce back reading from a February reading that was somewhat distorted due to the healthcare strike. Here’s everything to know about the March jobs report.

What Sectors Added Jobs In March?

On an overall basis, employers added 178,000 jobs in March, well above the 59,000 jobs expected. Job gains were strong throughout but most pronounced in the healthcare sector.

After losing jobs in February due to strike activity, the sector bounced back in a big way. The sector added 76,000 jobs during the month, well above the twelve month average of 29,000 jobs.

This wasn't a surprise to me, especially with the strike activity concluding. Gains, however, were better than expected elsewhere.

In the construction sector, for example, employers added 26,000 jobs. This was almost half the gains that were expected for the month. These gains were supplemented by a 21,000 job gain in the transportation and warehousing sector as well.

Two struggling sectors continue to be federal, which lost 18,000 jobs and financial activities, which saw payrolls decline 15,000. The latter could be the impacts rippling across financial services, with recently announced layoffs at companies such as Morgan Stanley (MS).

Unemployment and Wage Growth Trend

With the unemployment rate landing at 4.3%, the figure is largely in line with what we’ve seen over the past several months.

In my view, the labor market appears to remain in a narrow holding pattern. While the modest decline from February’s level is worth calling out, it does little to change the broader narrative that has defined this cycle.

Employers continue to show a clear hesitancy to meaningfully expand payrolls, but they appear to be showing an equally strong reluctance to part ways with existing workers on a large scale.

That push and pull has kept unemployment confined to a tight range even as overall job growth has cooled from prior years.

To me, I believe businesses remain in a wait-and-see mode. However, with more companies quietly announcing workforce reductions, it’s possible and maybe even likely that these cuts begin to surface more clearly in the data in the months ahead.

Fed Policy Implications

Much like in February, I don’t believe March’s jobs data will materially shift the near-term outlook for the Fed. If anything, market expectations have drifted even further toward the idea that policymakers could remain on hold for the entirety of 2026.

In my view, the logic is fairly straightforward. It now takes far fewer monthly job gains to keep the unemployment rate steady than it did even a year ago.

With the breakeven level for payroll growth estimated to be a fraction of prior thresholds, a report showing, say 50K to 70K jobs, would likely no longer be viewed as weak enough to force the Fed’s hand.

At the same time, however, geopolitical uncertainty, particularly surrounding the conflict with Iran, adds another layer of caution for policymakers.

I tend to agree with the growing market view. Unless unemployment begins to move meaningfully higher, I believe the Fed has ample room to remain steady and keep rates unchanged for longer than many initially expected.

Final Thoughts On The March Jobs Report

Today’s release was a good report for Good Friday.

The headline number showed a significant rebound from February’s distortion, healthcare hiring returned, and the unemployment rate remained pinned in its familiar mid-4% range.

With U.S. equity markets closed Friday, any true market reaction won’t occur until Monday. By then, however, I suspect most investors will have already shifted their focus back to developments surrounding the war with Iran rather than dissecting the finer details of today's payroll print, which was mostly a 'bounce back' reading.

That dynamic speaks to the broader investor view I outlined in my market outlook article from December 2025.

That is, selectivity likely remains paramount through 2026. Geopolitical conflicts, uneven sector hiring, and a Federal Reserve likely content to sit on its hands create an environment where momentum may struggle to do the heavy lifting.

For investors, that means continuing to be deliberate, selective, and disciplined in where capital is deployed.
